text.title: Multi-Machine Offline Cache Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Multi-Machine Offline Cache application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Each machine has an isolated cache directory + machine ID. Cross-machine sync is opt-in (rsync/Git/cloud) and metadata-aware.
rule.rationale: Shared cache via Dropbox / iCloud causes corruption + license-state confusion; explicit isolation is the safe default.

rule.statement: Cache uses content-addressable storage (CAS): files keyed by SHA-256. Stale entries detected on hash mismatch; not on file timestamps.
rule.rationale: Timestamp-based cache fails across machines (clock skew); CAS is the only reliable design.

rule.statement: Cache survives offline for ≥7 days. After 7 days, prompt for online refresh. Hard-block after 30 days unless explicit offline license.
rule.rationale: Balances usability (flights, no-internet client sites) against license auditability.

rule.statement: Each machine consumes one activation slot. Re-activation on a new machine requires deactivating an old one OR upgrading license tier.
rule.rationale: Naive licensing either fails legitimate users (no-mobile-license) or permits piracy; activation count is the industry-standard middle ground.

rule.statement: On license expiration, cache enters read-only mode; on hard-end, purge tier-gated content within 7 days.
rule.rationale: Long-tail license expiration creates piracy risk; explicit purge is the contractual safeguard.
